segments.s450-g9: بدو گفت گازر که بازآر هوش is a report clause: بدو (to him) marks the indirect object/recipient of گفت (said). که introduces the content of what was said. بازآر is an imperative/optative meaning “bring back” (literally ‘restore’), and هوش is the direct object meaning ‘sense/awareness’ — together “bring back your senses.” Word order places the recipient before the reporting verb and the reported clause after که, typical in Persian narrative style.
segments.s450-g10: ترا زشت باشد ازین پس خروش is the remainder of the reported admonition. ترا (تو + را) is the second‑person object (‘you’). زشت باشد is an optative/jussive expression ‘it is shameful/ unbecoming’ (باشد here has an optative sense). ازین پس is the temporal phrase ‘from now on’ (از + این/این orthographic variant), and خروش is the action judged shameful (‘cry/outcry’). The clause states that crying out is unbecoming for him henceforth.
segments.s450-g11: کنون‌گر بماند سخن در نهفت is a conditional subordinate clause. کنون‌گر is the fused form meaning ‘if now’ (کنون + گر, where گر = ‘if’). بماند is a present/subjunctive form ‘remains/should remain’. سخن is the thing in question (‘the word/matter’), and در نهفت means ‘in concealment/hidden’. Together: ‘if now the matter remains hidden.’
segments.s450-g12: بگویم به پیش سزاوار جفت is the main clause: بگویم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive (here future/intentional) ‘I will say/tell’. به پیش is a prepositional phrase ‘before/in the presence of’ and سزاوار qualifies the following noun as ‘fitting/appropriate’. جفت (‘pair’) is the addressee/ audience here: ‘I will tell it in front of the suitable pair.’ Object (سخن) is carried over from the subordinate clause.
segments.s450-g13: به سنگی که من جامه را برزنم is a locative relative clause. به سنگی = ‘on/against a stone’ (به used locatively), که introduces the relative clause ‘on which’. من is the subject ‘I’, جامه را is the definite object (را marks the garment), and برزنم is a first‑person verb ‘I will beat/wash’ (archaic/poetic form meaning to thrash/beat clothes on a stone). Object precedes verb, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s450-g14: چو پاکیزه گردد به آب افگنم is a temporal clause plus result. چو = ‘when’; پاکیزه گردد = ‘becomes clean’ (گردد = become, subjunctive/impersonal form). به آب is the directional phrase ‘into the water’, and افگنم is 1st‑person verb ‘I will throw/cast’ (archaic form). The structure is ‘when it is clean, I will cast it into the water.’
segments.s450-g15: دران جوی صندوق دیدم یکی is a straightforward locative and verbal clause. دران = در + آن = ‘in that’; جوی = ‘stream’; صندوق is the noun ‘chest/box’. دیدم is past‑tense ‘I saw’, and یکی is the indefinite numeral/article ‘one / a certain’. Word order places the locative phrase before the verb and the thing seen after it.
segments.s450-g16: نهفته بدو اندرون کودکی is a compact predicate describing what was inside the chest. نهفته = ‘hidden/concealed’; بدو = ‘to it / in it’ (archaic locative/dative pronoun referring back to صندوق); اندرون = ‘inside’; کودکی = ‘a child’. The phrase reads ‘hidden in it, a child’ (i.e., ‘a child hidden inside it’).
segments.s450-g121: "همان زخم چوگان و تیر و کمان" is a noun phrase in which همان ('that/same') prefaces زخم ('wound') and then a coordinated list of instruments follows: چوگان 'polo mallet', و 'and', تیر 'arrow', و 'and', کمان 'bow'. The phrase therefore names physical wounds inflicted by those implements; چوگان here means the mallet (not the game).
segments.s450-g122: هنرجوی is a nominal phrase meaning 'art‑seeker' or 'apprentice/trainee' acting as the subject; دور از is a prepositional phrase 'far from' and takes the following noun phrase بد بدگمان. بدگمان is an adjective 'suspicious/distrustful' and here is preceded by بد as an intensifier ('bad/ill‑'), so the whole means 'far from (any) evil suspicion' or 'free from ill distrust.' The verb is omitted (understood 'was')—a common poetic ellipsis.
segments.s450-g123: This clause sets up a result: بران گونه شد 'it became/occurred in that manner' — بران is a poetic adverbial ('thereupon/thereon'), گونه شد is 'became/turned out so', and زین هنرها literally 'of/among these arts/trappings' links the cause ('from these arts'). The conjunction که introduces the subordinate result clause that follows.
segments.s450-g124: چنگ نسودی should be read together as 'Nesudi's harp' (noun + epithet/name after it). به آورد is a past tense verbal phrase 'brought' (literally 'brought to/produced'), and با او پلنگ is the object phrase 'a leopard with it/him' (با او = 'with him/it' referring back to the harp or its owner). Word order is typical Persian: possessed noun + possessor, then verb, then object; the preposition با marks companionship/association.
segments.s450-g17: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause. من is the 1st-person subject and برگشادم is a past‑tense verb form (1st singular past) describing the narrator's action; the following noun phrase در بسته ('closed door') is predicative and the adjective/verb باز indicates its resulting state 'open'. Word order places the time-clause first and then the resulting state.
segments.s450-g18: به دیدار is a purposive prepositional phrase 'to/for the seeing/meeting of'; آن خردم is a direct object phrase 'that little one of mine' (خردم = my small one) and آمد is the past tense 'came' used with نیاز to form an idiom meaning 'a need/urge came (upon me)'. Overall it means 'I felt the need to see that little one.'
segments.s450-g19: اگر begins a conditional 'if'. بود is the past-tense copula 'there was/were' and with the dative/possessive phrase ما را it yields 'if there were for us' / 'if we had'. یکی پور خرد is a noun phrase 'one son (پور) of wisdom' (پور + خرد = a wise/valuable son). The possessor is expressed by the clitic construction ما را + بود.
segments.s450-g20: نبودش is a compact past/negative form meaning 'he/it were not' or 'his absence' (the -ش suffix refers back to the son). بسی here intensifies ('many/much') and زندگانی 'life(s)'; بمرد is a subjunctive/optative past-form meaning 'would die' or 'would perish' in this conditional context. Together: 'in his absence many lives would perish.'
segments.s450-g21: کنون is an adverb 'now'. یافتی is a past (2nd-person) form 'you found/obtained' (poetic narration often uses older endings). پور is 'son' and با خواسته is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with/according to the wish' (خواسته used adjectivally 'as desired').
segments.s450-g22: به introduces the instruments/materials and دینار و دیبا list them ('dinars and brocade'). بیاراسته is a past-participial/adjectival form meaning 'arrayed/adorned' (here describing the son). The whole phrase says he is clothed/adorned with money and fine cloth.
segments.s450-g23: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. آن جامه‌ها 'those garments' is the object, بر زمین 'on the ground' is the locative phrase, and بر نهاد is the past tense verb 'placed/laid upon' (نهاد with the prefix بر‑ giving the sense 'put upon'). Word order is typical poetic: time → object → place → verb.
segments.s450-g24: سر تنگ صندوق is a genitive chain 'the tight/narrow top (سر تنگ) of the chest (صندوق)' with را marking the object. برگشاد is the past tense 'opened' (he opened). The clause therefore means 'he opened the chest's tight lid.'
segments.s450-g41: This short clause names the agent and the manner of the raising. زن (zan) is the subject 'the woman'; گازر (Gāzar) is a proper name in apposition to زن (not one of the listed senses, so treated here as a name). او را uses the object pronoun او 'him/her' plus the definite/object marker را. چو 'as/like' introduces a simile; پیوند خویش is a possessive phrase 'the bond/connection of her own' (پیوند = 'bond/link') with the pronominal clitic ش = 'her'. Word order is typical for classical Persian: subject — (name in apposition) — object with را — simile phrase.
segments.s450-g42: بپرورد is a verbal form meaning 'he/she raised' or 'nurtured' (here 3rd person past/action in narrative; the initial ب- is an old/poetic prefix found in Ferdowsi). چونانک 'just as' strengthens the comparison. فرزند خویش 'her own child' repeats the possessive idea with فرزند 'child' and the clitic ش = 'her'. The clause completes the simile started by چو.
segments.s450-g43: سیم روز (here 'after thirty days' — an adverbial time phrase) precedes the verb as is common in Persian. داراب کردند نام is an inverted/poetic order of the usual نام کردند داراب or نامش داراب کردند: کردند is the 3rd person plural past 'they made/did', and نام is the noun 'name' used with کردن to mean 'to name (someone)'. The proper name داراب stands before the verb as the object of the naming.
segments.s450-g44: کز is the archaic/poetic relative/conjunction 'that/for' introducing the reason. آب روان is a compound 'flowing water' (آب 'water' + روان 'flowing'). یافتندش is the 3rd person plural past 'they found' with the enclitic object -ش 'him'. کنام is a locative/poetic noun 'in the lap/bosom' or 'at the side of' and here functions as a locative complement showing where he was found.
segments.s450-g45: چنان بد که is a fixed classical pattern: چنان 'so/thus' + بد 'bad/ill/unfavorable' + که introduces the resulting clause ('so that'). روزی here means 'one day/once' (temporal adverb). زن پاک‌رای places the adjective after the noun in poetic diction: زن 'the woman' + پاک‌رای 'of pure reputation / virtuous'.
segments.s450-g46: سخن گفت is the verbal expression 'spoke / held speech' (سخن here names the act of speaking; گفت is the past form 'said/spoke'). هرگونه 'in any way / in every manner' modifies how she spoke. با + کدخدای marks the interlocutor: 'with the kadhkhudā' (village headman). Word order is natural: verb phrase (speak) followed by manner adverb then the prepositional object.
segments.s450-g47: که at the start here is a conjunction introducing reported speech or a question. این گوهران را is the direct object 'these jewels' with the object marker را. چه 'what' is the interrogative; سازی is the verbal/nominal form 'making/doing' (in context equivalent to 'what will you do / what will you make of (them)') — in classical usage سازی here expresses 'you will do/make' (a second-person sense in the idiom چه سازی). کنون 'now' is an adverb placing the action in time.
segments.s450-g48: که here resumes the subordinate clause 'so that / that' (introducing purpose or result). باشد is a present-subjunctive/formal 'may be' (3rd person singular). بدین is the poetic prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (به + این contracted in poetry). دانشت is the possessive expression 'your knowledge' (دانش + ـت), and رهنمون is the noun 'guide / guidance'. Put together: 'that this may be a guide to your knowledge.'
segments.s450-g57: ببردند is the past 3rd-person plural of بردن ('to take/lead'), here 'they took/led away'. داراب را is the definite object (را marks Darab as the direct object). در کنار is a locative phrase 'to the side / aside' placed after the object; the verb-before-object order is a poetic inversion but the grammar remains past-tense narrative.
segments.s450-g58: نکردند is the negative past (they did not do); paired with جز it yields the sense 'they did nothing except / left nothing but'. جز introduces the exception. گوهر و زر are the items excepted (jewels and gold). به بار ('as a load/cargo') qualifies what was taken/left—بار is 'load, cargo' and به marks that role.
segments.s450-g59: بپیمود is past 3rd-person singular of پیمودن ('to traverse, span'); it narrates that he covered a distance. زان = 'from that / of that', linking to 'مرز' (the frontier/border). فرسنگ شست gives the distance in parasangs; the final word شست here is a numeral/measure in context (archaic), so the sequence means 'for X parasangs'. Word order places the verb before its locative phrase (poetic inversion).
segments.s450-g60: به + شهری دگر = 'to / in another city' (به as locative/towards). ساخت is past 'he built/made'. جای نشست is a coordinated idea: جای 'a place' and نشست 'he sat / settled' — together 'he built a place and settled (there)'. نشست is past 'sat/settled'.
segments.s450-g61: به بیگانه شهر اندرون = 'in/at the foreign city, the inner part' (به marks location; بیگانه qualifies شهر as 'foreign/alien'; اندرون 'interior/inner quarters'). ساخت جای = past 'he built a place/quarters' (ساخت is 'built', جای 'place'). The sequence describes setting up inner dwellings in that foreign city.
segments.s450-g62: بران سان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in that manner / thereby' (بران = 'thereon/on that', سان = 'like/so'). که introduces the result clause 'that...'. پرمایه‌تر is the comparative form ('more substantial / richer'), modifying کدخدای ('the village headman'). Grammatically this is a result/comparative clause: 'so that the headman became richer.'
segments.s450-g63: به شهری که introduces a locative + relative clause: 'in/to a city that...'. بد نامور is a compound adjective 'ill‑famed / notorious' describing the city’s chief; مهتری is the noun 'the chief/elder' (here 'the city's chief' in the relative clause). The relative clause modifies شهری.
segments.s450-g64: فرستاد is past 3rd-person singular 'he sent' (the implied subject is the chief of that city). نزدیک او functions as 'to him / toward him' (نزدیک here = 'near/unto' with او 'him'). گوهری is the object 'a jewel'. Word order places the verb before its indirect object and the object noun at the end, typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s450-g65: ازو is a source pronoun ‘from it / from her’ placed before the verb for emphasis. بستدی is an archaic past 3rd‑person singular form of بستن (to bind/fasten/tie up), here transitive and taking جامه و سیم و زر as its direct objects; the three nouns are coordinated by و in the normal enumerative order.
segments.s450-g66: چنین is a discourse adverb ‘thus/so’. تا introduces a purposive/result clause ‘so that / in order that’. فراوان نماند is a negative purposive clause: نماند (3rd‑person subjunctive/negative present here) ‘not remain / be left’ with فراوان as its adverbial complement; از گهر (“of/on the gems”) marks the domain from which nothing remains.
segments.s450-g67: به خانه is a locative prepositional phrase ‘in/at the house’ placed up front for topicality. جز از introduces an exception (‘except from’), followed by سرخ گوگرد ‘red brimstone’ as the excluded item; نیز here simply reinforces ‘also / even’. The word order (prepositional phrase → exception → item) is typical of epic/poetic fronting.
segments.s450-g68: نماند is the main verb ‘there remained / was left’ (3rd‑person), followed by a genitive/partitive phrase از بد و نیک (‘of bad and good’) describing the kinds of things, and then صندوق چیز ‘chest‑thing’ (i.e. ‘thing in the chest’). The overall meaning: nothing remained in the chest, whether bad or good.
segments.s450-g69: زن گازر is a noun phrase where گازر (from گزار/گزارش) functions as an agentive/attributive ‘the woman who reported / the reporting woman’. از چیز is a genitive phrase ‘of the matter / about the thing’. شد is the past auxiliary/lexical verb ‘became’, and رهنمای is the predicate nominative ‘a guide / adviser’ linked by شد.
segments.s450-g70: چنین is a discourse marker ‘so / thus’. گفت here functions as the speech verb ‘said’ (the lexical sense is a spoken-form verb though the listed lexeme senses are nominalized). یک روز is a temporal adverbial ‘one day’, and با کدخدای is the prepositional phrase ‘to/with the headman’ indicating the addressee of the speech.
segments.s450-g71: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ‘that / for’. ما is the subject ‘we’. بی‌نیازیم is a stative predicate ‘we are free of need / self‑sufficient’ (first‑person plural). زین کارکرد is a prepositional/genitive phrase ‘from this deed/action’; زین is an archaic form equivalent to ازِ این (‘from this’), and کارکرد here means ‘act / performance’.
segments.s450-g72: توانگر شدی is a 2nd‑person past/perfect ‘you have become wealthy’ (addressing the addressee). گرد پیشه is an expression ‘about/around your trade’ (گرد ‘around/about’ with پیشه ‘profession’). مگرد is a prohibitive/negative command here meaning ‘do not turn / do not go back’ (in context: ‘do not resume / do not go about your craft’), addressed to the same person.
segments.s450-g25: This clause describes the observer and her reaction. 'زن گازر' is a noun phrase meaning 'a passing woman' (noun + attributive), 'آن' is a demonstrative pronoun ('that' / 'it'), and 'دید' is the simple past verb 'saw/observed'. 'خیره بماند' is a predicative phrase: 'خیره' (staring/astonished) + 'بماند' (here used as a past-narrative/preterite sense 'remained' in classical poetic style, with the prefixed بـ that appears often in older Persian). Word order is typical for Ferdowsi: observer + object + verb + result-state.
segments.s450-g26: 'بروبر' is an adverbial modifier meaning roughly 'directly/straightaway' or 'face-to-face' that modifies the verb. 'جهان‌آفرین را' is the direct object with the Persian object marker 'را' ('the Creator' = God/the world‑maker). 'بخواند' is 3rd‑person singular past (classical form) meaning 'called out to' or 'cried aloud to' — here 'بخواند' = 'she called/summoned/cried to'.
segments.s450-g27: Literal order: 'رخی' (a face/countenance) + 'دید' (saw) + 'تابان' (shining) + 'میان حریر' (among the silk). Classical Persian often places the descriptive adjective ('تابان') after the verb. Here 'رخی' functions as a noun 'a countenance' and 'تابان' qualifies it ('a shining countenance'). 'میان حریر' gives the locative setting ('amid/among silk').
segments.s450-g28: 'به دیدار ماننده اردشیر' is a simile of appearance: 'به' here = 'in' (indicating the mode or aspect), 'دیدار' = 'the sight/appearance', 'ماننده' = 'like/similar to', and 'اردشیر' is the proper name. Together: 'in appearance like Ardashir' — i.e. the face resembled Ardashir's.
segments.s450-g29: Structure: 'پر' = 'full (of)' + 'از' (of/from) + 'در' (here: vessels/containers) + 'خوشاب' ('pleasant drink' / sweet water) followed by 'بالین او' ('by his pillow' or 'at his bedside'). The phrase thus describes containers of pleasant drink located at his pillow; 'بالین' can mean 'pillow' or 'at the bedside' so the line deliberately places refreshments beside him.
segments.s450-g30: This is a short locative list: 'عقیق و زبرجد' (agate and peridot) followed by 'به پایین او' — 'beneath him' or 'under his lower side'. 'به' here introduces the spatial relation and 'پایین' functions as 'below/under'. The simple past/copula is elided across the two coordinated parts in the line.
segments.s450-g31: 'به دست چپش' is a locative phrase 'at/on his left hand' — note the enclitic possessive '-ش' on 'چپ' marking 'his'. 'سرخ دینار بود' places the noun phrase 'a red dinar' before the verb; 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. Classical Persian commonly uses adjective + noun + 'بود' for existence statements in narrative.
segments.s450-g32: 'سوی راست' = 'on the right side' (sui/side + راست/right). 'یاقوت شهوار' is a compound: 'یاقوت' (ruby) followed by 'شهوار' — an epithet meaning 'kinglike' or 'fit for a king' — placed after the noun as an adjectival epithet in the classical style. 'بود' again is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s450-g89: بدو گفت is a fixed sequence meaning “he said to him” (بدو = to him + گفت = said). کاین introduces a subordinate clause (“that/this”) and جامه برزن is a genitive/attributive phrase: جامه (garment) modified by برزن (of the plain / countryman). به + سنگ is a simile/comparison (“like a stone”); به before a noun often marks the comparison.
segments.s450-g90: که begins the explanatory subordinate clause (“that / for which”). از پیشه جستن is an infinitival phrase meaning “to seek (a living) from a craft/profession” (از marks source). ت + را here together form the object ‘ترا’ (“you”) with ت the pronominal element and را the object marker. نیست ننگ is a nominal predicate with نیست (“is not”) + ننگ (“shame”) = “is not shameful / it is no disgrace.”
segments.s450-g91: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” داراب is the personal name (subject). زان = ز + آن, “from that / from it,” here marking the source (زان پیشه = ‘from that profession’). بگریختی is a past verb meaning “(he) fled”; in epic/older Persian the -ی ending appears where modern Persian uses -َ or -د, so it functions as a past tense agreeing with the proper-name subject.
segments.s450-g92: همی is an iterative adverb “continually / constantly.” گازر here functions as the subject (a personal name or an agent described as a passer). از دیده is a prepositional phrase “from the eye(s),” خون ریختی is a past clause literally “poured blood” = “shed blood / wept blood”; ریختی is the past of ریختن in this poetic form.
segments.s450-g93: شدی روزگارش = “his fortune became / his condition came to be.” به جستن is an infinitival phrase “in/for seeking,” where به links the verb with its circumstance. دو بهر = “two (b)ehar” = “for two purposes / in two ways”; the numeral precedes the noun as usual.
segments.s450-g94: نشان خواستی = “you sought a sign/trace” (نشان = trace/sign; خواستی = past of خواستن “to want/seek”). زو is ز + او = “of/from him.” به دشت و به شهر are locative prepositional phrases “in the plain and in the city,” with the preposition به repeated for each coordinate.
segments.s450-g95: به جاییش is a locative phrase “in some place / at one place” (به + جایِ + -ش). دیدی is second-person past “you saw.” کمانی is an adjective derived from کمان used substantively here = “(a) bow.” به دست is the locative/instrumental “in/at (the) hand / in hand.”
segments.s450-g96: به آیین = “according to custom/practice” (به + آیین). گشاده ... بسته شست is a nominal phrase with omitted copula: “(the) hand/palm open and (the) thumb closed.” In poetic word order the adjectives precede the implied noun; بر here is a short classical particle/locative sense (‘upon / forward’) linking the description—overall meaning is a conventional posture “open hand and closed thumb.”
segments.s450-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. بیگاه is an adverb 'at dawn' placed before the subject; گازر is the subject 'the passer'; بیامد is the past tense verb 'came'; ز رود is a prepositional phrase 'from the river' with poetic ز = 'from'. Word order is typical classical Persian: conjunction + time adverb + subject + verb + origin.
segments.s450-g2: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (be + 3rd‑person object). جفت او = 'his companion/partner' (جفت = mate) functions as the agent of گفت; here گفت functions as the past verb 'said' (the provided lexical senses list treats گفت oddly as 'conversation', but in context it is the past-tense verb 'said'). هست این درود is a copular clause quoted after the speech-verb: هست (copula 'is') + این درود ('this greeting'); the order is slightly marked (copula before the demonstrative), giving the formulaic reported speech 'he said: “this is a greeting.”'.
segments.s450-g3: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('since/that'); باز آمدی is second‑person past 'you returned' (باز = 'again/back', آمدی = 'you came'); جامه‌ها نیم‌نم is a nominal predicate 'the garments (are) slightly damp' with the copula omitted (classical Persian often drops هست/بود in such descriptive clauses).
segments.s450-g4: بدین کارکرد = 'by/with this action' where ب- functions as the prepositional prefix 'by/with' and دین here belongs to the classical compound بدین ('with this'); کارکرد is 'action/deed'. از که یابی درم is an interrogative phrase 'from whom will you obtain a dirham?' with از = 'from', که = 'who/whom' and یابی = 2nd‑person present/future 'you will find/obtain' (classical present used for future sense). Note: the tokenization split بدین into ب + دین; دین as a separate lemma does not carry the sense 'this' by itself here, so its standalone senses do not match the function (hence marked below as -1).
segments.s450-g5: دل گازر = 'the passer's heart' (possessive noun phrase). از درد is a causal prepositional phrase 'from/of pain/grief' and پژمرده بود is the past‑state predicate 'was withered/forlorn' (پژمرده = faded/dejected, بود = past copula 'was'). The clause describes an emotional state caused by sorrow.
segments.s450-g6: یکی = 'a certain' (introducing an indefinite item). کودک زیرکش = 'a child (in an) undershirt/undergarment' — زیرکش modifies کودک and follows it (post‑nominal modifier or descriptive adjunct). مرده بود is the past 'was dead' (murdé بود = 'had died/was dead').
segments.s450-g7: زن گازر = 'the passer's wife' (possessive noun phrase). از درد کودک نوان = 'from/with grief at the young child' where از indicates cause, کودک = 'child', and نوان is an adjective 'young/youthful' modifying کودک. The whole phrase states the cause of her distress.
segments.s450-g8: This segment describes her resulting condition. خلیده is a past participial/state verb meaning roughly 'having sunk/withdrawn/overcome' (the provided senses do not list this exact shade, so its lexical sense is annotated as unavailable below). رخان here refers to her visage/face/expression (the given senses treat رخان as an adjective like 'loose/soft', but in context it functions as part of a phrase describing the face). تیره گشته = 'has become dark' (تیره = 'dark', گشته = perfect participle 'become'); روان = 'spirit/soul', so the clause says her countenance sank and her spirit became dark or fled. Grammatically, گشته is a perfect participle showing resultant state.
segments.s450-g97: کمان بستدی is a past tense (2nd‑person) verbal phrase: کمان (object) + بستدی (past ‘you closed/strung’); Persian often omits the explicit subject. سرد follows the verb and is used adverbially (‘coldly’ or ‘sternly’) to qualify گفتی, which here is the 2nd‑person past ‘you said’. بدوی is an archaic/poetic form and is syntactically attached as part of what was said (vocative/interjection); its exact lexical match is uncertain in the provided senses.
segments.s450-g98: که‌ای begins the quotation/address: که (relative/that/who) + ای (vocative particle) — here introducing the words reported. پرزیان is a tribal/name noun used vocatively (‘O Parziyan’); گرگ is the noun ‘wolf’ and پرخاشجوی is the post‑nominal adjective ‘aggressive/quarrelsome’ modifying گرگ (classical Persian places adjectives after the noun). Together they form the content of the quoted insult.
segments.s450-g99: چه is an interrogative/expressive particle (‘why/how’). گردی here denotes wandering/going about (a verbal/nominal form), and همی is the older continuous particle meaning ‘still/continually’. گرد is the verbal idea ‘to go about’, while تیر و کمان is the prepositional/oblique accompaniment (‘with arrow and bow’), placed after the verb phrase in characteristic epic word order.
segments.s450-g100: به خردی is a prepositional phrase literally ‘with regard to smallness’ (here ironical: ‘in your wisdom’ or ‘over trivial things’). چرا is the interrogative ‘why’, گشته‌ای is 2nd‑person perfect/past participial use ‘have you become’ or ‘you have become’, and بدگمان is a predicate adjective ‘suspicious/distrustful’. The clause asks why the addressee has grown distrustful about petty matters.
segments.s450-g101: به گازر is a dative prepositional phrase ‘to the passer/bypasser’ (به + noun). چنین گفت is a fixed reportive sequence ‘said thus/so’ (گفت is the past tense ‘he said’). کای is an archaic vocative particle ‘O’, and باب من is a vocative noun phrase ‘my father’ (باب here is an archaic word for a close kin or a form of address; this meaning is not reflected in the provided senses).
segments.s450-g102: چرا is ‘why’. تیره گردانی is a causative/verbal phrase: تیره (dark/ominous) + گردانی (you make/you turn) — together ‘do you make (it) dark’ or ‘do you darken’. این آب من is the object ‘this my water’ (literal ‘this water of mine’, often figurative for one’s life/fortune). Word order places the interrogative first, verb in the middle, and the demonstrative + possessor after the verb.
segments.s450-g103: به فرهنگیان is a dative phrase ‘to the learned/men of learning’ (فرهنگیان = ‘men of culture/learning’). ده is the imperative ‘give’ (from دادن). مرا is the object ‘me’ (in the data split as م + را; together they form the accusative ‘mear/a’). از نخست means ‘from the first’ or ‘from the beginning’ and modifies the imperative phrase.
segments.s450-g104: چو is a subordinating particle here meaning ‘as’ or ‘when’. آموختم is past tense ‘I learned’ (classical ambiguity with ‘I was taught’—context favors ‘as I was taught/learned’). زند and استا are technical/lexical items (Zand = a type of commentary; استا is an older term here treated as a named item) linked by و ‘and’; درست is an adverb/adjective ‘correct/true’ qualifying them. The clause says that, according to what he learned/taught, these works/things are correct.
segments.s450-g73: 'چنین' is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' modifying the verb that follows. 'داد' is the simple past 'gave' (third-person singular). 'پاسخ' is the direct object 'answer/reply' and 'بدو' is the enclitic-like old-fashioned form for 'به او' (to him). 'کدخدای' (village-head) appears after the verb phrase and functions as the agent/subject; classical Persian often places the subject after the verb or verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s450-g74: 'که‌ای' here functions as an archaic/poetic vocative (roughly 'که ای' = 'O'), calling the addressee. 'جفت' is the noun 'pair' (addressed as a unit), followed by the adjectives 'پاکیزه' (pure/clean) and the coordinating 'و' ('and'), then 'رهنمای' 'guide'. Word order is noun + adjective(s), and the whole segment is a direct address in vocative form.
segments.s450-g75: 'همی' is a classical habitual/continuous marker (here 'you keep/always'). 'پیشه خوانی' is a verbal expression in which 'پیشه' (craft/profession) is combined with the verb 'خوانی' (2nd-person singular present/habitual); in context it means 'you perform/proclaim/practice your profession.' 'ز پیشه' is 'from/of the craft' and 'چه بیش' is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what more?' or 'what then?' — 'چه' here is an exclamatory/interrogative particle and 'بیش' means 'more.'
segments.s450-g76: 'همیشه' is an adverb 'always.' 'ز هر کار' is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from/of every deed.' 'پیشه است پیش' uses the copula 'است' with the subject 'پیشه' (craft) and the adverb 'پیش' ('before, ahead'): literally 'the craft is before' — i.e., 'the craft/profession comes ahead of every action.' Note the somewhat inverted classical word order where the copula sits between subject and adverbial complement.
segments.s450-g77: 'تو' is the emphatic/topic pronoun 'you.' 'داراب را' uses the object marker 'را' to mark Darab as the definite object. 'پاک و نیکو' are postpositive adjectives modifying Darab ('pure and good'). 'بدار' is an imperative singular (classical) meaning 'keep/hold' (from داشتن) — a command: 'keep Darab pure and good.'
segments.s450-g78: The word 'بدان' is built from the prefix 'ب' (here the classical imperative prefix) + 'دان' (root 'to know'), forming the imperative 'know' or 'be aware.' 'تا' introduces the content/purpose clause 'so that/what' and here leads into the interrogative content 'چه بار آورد روزگار' — 'what burden/occasion/time fate will bring.' 'آورد' is third-person past 'brings/brought' and 'روزگار' (literally 'times') functions as 'fortune/fate' here. The whole phrase means 'know (or see) what fate will bring.'
segments.s450-g79: 'همی' is the habitual/continuous marker again. 'داشتندش' is past tense plural 'they had/held him,' with the enclitic object 'ش' = 'him' attached. 'چنان ارجمند' is a result/complement phrase 'so esteemed/valued' — together: 'they held him so esteemed.'
segments.s450-g80: 'که' introduces a result clause ('such that...'). 'از تند بادی' = 'from the fierce wind' (preposition 'از' + adjective 'تند' 'fierce' + 'بادی' 'windy/wind-related'). 'ندیدی گزند' is an archaic/impersonal negative past construction literally 'one did not see harm' or 'no harm was seen' (here 'ندیدی' functions impersonally/past-negative rather than a literal second-person 'you did not see'), and 'گزند' is 'harm.' So the clause means 'that from a fierce wind no harm was seen/made.'
segments.s450-g49: This clause has an inverted order: the indirect object/adressee 'به زن' (to the woman) comes first, then the verb 'گفت' (said), and finally the speaker's name 'گازر' (Gāzar). The vocative phrase 'که‌ای نیک جفت' (literally 'O good mate') is an address to the woman — 'که‌ای' is an older/exclamatory form used here as 'O' + 'نیک جفت' 'good spouse/partner'.
segments.s450-g50: This is an interrogative clause introduced by the emphatic 'چه ... چه ...' (lit. 'what ... and what ...') asking in which place the speaker was hidden. 'خاک' and 'گوهرمرا' (literally 'my gem') are the two alternatives, 'در' marks the locative 'in/inside', and 'نهفت' is a past-state adjective/verb meaning 'hidden' (here used as 'did you hide'). The implied subject of 'نهفت' is the addressee (the woman).
segments.s450-g51: 'همان' here works as an adverb meaning 'at once' / 'immediately'; 'به کزین شهر بیرون شویم' is a purpose/optative-like sequence meaning 'that we go out of/from this city'. 'شویم' is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive form of 'شدن/شد' used here as a hortative/optative 'let us go/let us become out' (classical style for 'let us go out').
segments.s450-g52: This continuation gives the motive: 'ز تنگی و سختی' = 'because of/out of (the) narrowness and hardship'; the preposition 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' (from/because of). 'به هامون شویم' parallels the previous verb phrase: 'let us be/go to the plain' (هامون = a plain). Again 'شویم' expresses the speaker's wish/intent for first‑person plural.
segments.s450-g53: 'به شهری' = 'to/a city'; the relative clause 'که ما را ندانند کس' = 'where no one knows us.' Note the classical word order: the object marker 'را' follows 'ما' and 'ندانند' is the 3rd-person plural present (they do not know/recognize). 'کس' (anyone/no one) is placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s450-g54: This clause continues describing that city: 'که خواریم' = 'where we are humiliated/despised' (first‑person plural predication). The coordination 'و ناشادگر دست رس' compresses two ideas: 'ناشادگر' is an adjective 'saddening' or 'that causes sorrow', and 'دست رس' (literally 'hand-reach') is a compound notion meaning 'aid at hand' or 'help reachable' — here the combined sense conveys lack of consolation/help being available. The clause is terse and characteristic of epic diction.
segments.s450-g55: The temporal/adverbial phrase 'به شبگیر' signals 'at night' / 'by night' or describes a night‑visiting figure; 'گازر' (the passer) is the subject. 'بنه' here is a small bundle or burden and 'برنهاد' is a classical past form meaning 'placed upon / took up' — together 'took up the bundle'.
segments.s450-g56: This clause narrates his departure: 'برفت' is an archaic/periphrastic past meaning 'he went away' (the printed lexical senses given do not show this verbal use, so the mapping below marks that). 'و نکرد از بر و بوم یاد' = 'and he did not leave/produce any remembrance of home and homeland' — literally 'did not make (produce) remembrance from/of home and land.' The negative 'نکرد' governs 'یاد' as the object.
segments.s450-g105: ازان پس is a temporal phrase = “after that/thereupon.” مرا + را is the object pronoun with the object marker (here written as separate pieces in the segment); پیشه فرمان is a noun phrase “the craft/profession of command” (پیشه = profession, فرمان in genitive). جوی is an archaic imperative (2nd person singular) of جستن “seek” — here it orders the addressee to pursue that craft.
segments.s450-g106: کنون is an adverb “now.” از من = “from me” (preposition + object). این کدخدایی is a noun phrase “this kâdkhudâ‑ship / this village‑headship.” مجوی is a negative imperative (classical/poetic form) meaning “do not seek” (2nd person singular); the negative here is expressed in the verbal form rather than a separate negative particle.
segments.s450-g107: بدو is a prepositional phrase meaning “to him” (ب = to/into + دان in this orthography), placed before the subject. مرد گازر is a noun phrase ‘a man (named) Gazar’ (گازر here taken as a personal name or passer); بسی is an adverbial ‘much/many’ and برشمرد is a past tense verb ‘enumerated / recounted.’ The order (prep → subject → adverb → verb) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s450-g108: ازان پس again = “after that/thereupon.” به فرهنگیانش سپرد is a verb phrase: به + indirect object (فرهنگیانش = “his instructors/educators,” with the possessive suffix -ش) + سپرد (past tense of سپردن, “entrusted/handed over”). The verb comes at the end, as expected.
segments.s450-g109: بیاموخت is a past tense verb meaning “he learned” (the prefix بیـ is an older/perfective marker here). فرهنگ is the direct object “learning/culture.” و شد برمنش joins clauses: شد is the copular/auxiliary “became” and برمنش is a predicative adjective/adverbial phrase “noble‑minded / of good disposition.”
segments.s450-g110: برآمد is a past verb “arose/issued forth.” ز (poetic form of از) + پیغاره = “from the message/notice.” و سرزنش links the following nominal “and reproach”; syntactically the clause expresses that reproach arose from that message (verb + ز‑phrase + patient/theme). Note the verb precedes its source in this poetic inversion.
segments.s450-g111: بدان is an archaic/localized sequence here meaning “to that” (written as ب + دان in the tokenization); پروراننده is the addressee ‘the nurturer’ or caretaker. گفت is the verb “said” introducing reported speech, and ای پدر is the vocative “O father.” Word order is ‹to‑that nurturer› + ‹said› + ‹vocative› — the vocative is the direct address inside the reported speech.
segments.s450-g112: نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive/optative form “does not / will not come” (classical/poetic negative form). ز من = “from me.” گازری is a nisba/adjectival or proper‑name form related to گازر (here used as a substantive or qualifier) and کارگر functions adjectivally as “able/effective” rather than merely ‘worker’ — together meaning roughly “no able (one of Gazar) will come from me” or more generally “no capable worker will come from me.”
segments.s450-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. برگشت is the past verb 'turned/returned' and follows the subject چرخ ('wheel' = wheel of fortune); از + برش (از + بر+ـش) means 'from his side' — بر + ـش is a possessive/relational formation meaning 'his side/profile' (poetic). چند سال is a postposed time-phrase 'for several years'.
segments.s450-g82: یکی here is the numeral 'one/a single'; کودکی is a noun meaning 'a child' (the -ی nominal form), and گشت is the past of شدن 'became' (stative/resultative). با + فر و یال is an instrumental/comitative phrase 'with (royal) glory and a mane' — typical epic epithets where فر = divine/royal splendour and یال = mane/crest.
segments.s450-g83: به + کشتی here marks the activity/field — 'to wrestling' (کشتی = wrestling). شدی is a past 'became/took up' used poetically (the -ی ending is an archaic/poetic third‑person past form). با بزرگان is accompaniment 'with the great ones', and به کوی gives the location 'in/to the quarter/neighbourhood.'
segments.s450-g84: کسی را ... نبودی is a negative-existential construction: کسی را (indefinite object marked with را) followed by نبودی ('was not' = ن + بودی) yields 'there was no one'. تن و زور اوی is the possessed phrase 'his body and strength' (اوی = his, a poetic possessive form). Word order places the quantified/indefinite noun before the verb, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s450-g85: همه is the quantifier 'all' placed before the noun کودکان ('children'), and همگروه is a postpositive attribute 'fellow-member / of the same group'. آمدند is the plural past 'they came', agreeing with the plural subject.
segments.s450-g86: به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase 'suddenly'. زو is a contracted poetic form of از او ('from/of him'); here it expresses cause ('because of / of him') — the text splits it into ز + و, where و stands in place of the pronoun element. ستوه آمدند is an idiomatic predicate 'they became weary/distressed' (ستوه as adjective + آمدند 'they came to be').
segments.s450-g87: به فریاد شد is a verbal expression 'became a cry / rose up as an outcry' with به marking the resultant state. گازر here functions as 'report/rumour' (not merely a passer-by) and is the thing that 'became an outcry'. از کار او gives the cause/ground 'because of his deed(s)'.
segments.s450-g88: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle 'indeed/still'; تیره شد is the change-of-state 'became dark/ominous'. تیز بازار is a compound where تیز modifies بازار — here 'ti:z' carries the sense of sharp/keen/swift (= brisk/busy) applied to the market; او is the possessive/third-person pronoun 'his' (his market).
segments.s450-g113: ز من جای مهرت is a prepositional/contrastive phrase: ز (poetic 'from/instead of') + من (object 'me') + جای مهرت ('in place of your favour' — جای + مهرت, with مهرت = 'your favour/kindness'). بی‌اندیشه کن is an imperative: بی‌اندیشه ('without thinking/hesitation', adverbial) + کن (imperative 'do/make'). The verb (کن) governs the whole preceding phrase: 'Make, without thought, (instead of me) your favour.'
segments.s450-g114: ز گیتی سواری مرا پیشه کن is another imperative clause. ز گیتی ('of/from the world' — poetic locative/relational) + سواری ('riding/horse-service') forms the thing to be made a پیشه ('profession, trade'). مرا is the object ('me' + object marker را split in the gloss), and کن is the imperative 'make'. Word order puts the domain (ز گیتی سواری) before the object and the verb: 'Make for me riding in the world my profession.'
segments.s450-g115: نگه کرد is a simple past verbal phrase 'he looked/gazed' (کرد used as past), followed by the nominal phrase گازر سواری تمام: گازر 'the passer/passerby' + سواری 'rider/horseman' + تمام 'whole/complete'. Poetic word order places the verb before the noun phrase: 'He looked — the passer/rider was wholly a horseman.'
segments.s450-g116: This is a string of coordinated participial/nominal descriptions of the rider's action/quality. عنان پیچ is 'reins/twisting' (literally 'a twist of the reins'), و joins phrases, اسپ افگن reads as 'the steed (اسپ) + افگن (shining/brightening)', and نیک‌نام is an adjectival epithet 'well‑reputed'. The clauses are paratactic qualities/actions: 'twisting the reins, making the horse shine, and (being) famous.'
segments.s450-g117: سپردش is past 'he entrusted/consigned' with the object clitic -ش ('him/it'). بدو is dative 'to him/unto that man'. روزگاری دراز is a temporal adverbial 'for a long time' (روزگاری = time/period, دراز = long). Together: 'He entrusted him to him for a long while.'
segments.s450-g118: بیاموخت here should be read as 'was taught/received instruction' (past/passive sense). هرچش = 'هر چه' = 'whatever/everything'. ب + دان in the segment form a bound element meaning 'to/for that' (badly split in the gloss; treat as 'بدان' = 'for that'). بد نیاز is a compound 'bad/harsh need' = 'dire necessity'. The whole: 'He was taught everything for that dire need.'
segments.s450-g119: A coordinated nominal list followed by the infinitive/verb داشتن: عنان و سنان و سپر ('reins and spear and shield') + داشتن ('to have' / 'having'). Functionally this names the equipment he possessed: 'having reins, spear and shield.'
segments.s450-g120: به آوردگه is a locative 'to/at the arena/place of bringing' (به = 'to/for', آوردگه = 'venue/place'). باره برگاشتن is an infinitival/verbal phrase: باره (here 'load/gear') + برگاشتن ('to lift/take up' or 'to carry away'). Together: 'to take/carry the gear to the arena/place.'
segments.s450-g33: Word order is inverted: بدو (to him) precedes the verb داد (gave), so literally 'to him gave the woman quickly the breast of milk.' زن is the agent 'the woman'; زود qualifies the giving ('quickly'); پستان شیر is a noun phrase 'milk-bearing breast' (pستان = breast, شیر = milk).
segments.s450-g34: ببد is a past/result verb 'became/was turned' (here 'became'); شاد is the resulting state 'glad/happy.' زان = 'from that / because of that' links this clause to the previous action; کودک is the affected noun 'the child' and دلپذیر describes the child as 'delightful/pleasant.'
segments.s450-g35: ز here introduces a causal sense 'because of.' خوبی آن کودک = 'the goodness/virtue of that child.' و خواسته links 'and (the) wish' — خواسته is a noun 'wish/desire' (the heart's desire).
segments.s450-g36: دل او = 'his heart.' ز غم = 'from sorrow' (ز = 'from'). گشت is the past/result verb 'became' and پیراسته means 'purged/cleansed' (i.e., freed from sorrow). The clause states the heart's resulting state after the preceding cause.
segments.s450-g37: بدو 'to him' precedes گفت + گازر: word order is inverted — '[to] him said the passerby.' که introduces the content clause 'that'; این را marks 'this' as the definite object (را); به جان is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'with (our) lives' or 'by one's life' used to intensify commitment.
segments.s450-g38: خریدار is literal 'buyer' used metaphorically 'one who takes/cherishes'; باشیم is a subjunctive/volitive 'let us be / we should be' (present-subjunctive form used to express intent or exhortation); تا جاودان 'so that/to the end of being eternal' — تا often means 'so that' or 'until' here implying perpetuity ('forever').
segments.s450-g39: که introduces a clause giving a reason or assertion 'that'; این کودک 'this child'; نامداری is the abstract noun 'renown/fame'; بود is the past copula 'was' used to state that quality (here rendered as 'would be/was famed').
segments.s450-g40: گر introduces a concessive/conditional 'if / even if'; او is 'he'; در جهان 'in the world'; شهریاری means 'sovereignty/rule' (i.e., being a ruler); بود is again the past copula—together the clause expresses 'even if he were sovereign in the world.'

sentences.s450-s5: Gāzar said to him, “Bring back your senses — it is shameful for you to cry out from now on.”
sentences.s450-s6: If the matter remains concealed now, I will tell it before the fitting pair.
sentences.s450-s7: On a stone where I will beat the garment; when it becomes clean I will cast it into the water.
sentences.s450-s8: In that stream I saw a chest; inside it a child hidden.
sentences.s450-s61: Those same wounds from the polo mallet and from arrow and bow — the trainee (or skill-seeker) was far from evil suspicion.
sentences.s450-s62: From these arts it so came about that Nesudi's harp brought a leopard with it.
sentences.s450-s9: When I came back the closed door was open; I felt the need to go and see that little one of mine.
sentences.s450-s10: If we had one wise son, many lives would perish in his absence.
sentences.s450-s11: Now you have found a son as you wished, adorned with dinars and brocade.
sentences.s450-s12: When he laid those garments upon the ground, he opened the tight lid of the chest.
sentences.s450-s21: The woman Gāzar reared him as if he were of her own kin, nurturing him like her own child.
sentences.s450-s22: After thirty days they named him Darab, for they had found him in the flowing water.
sentences.s450-s23: It was so bad that one day the virtuous woman spoke in every possible way with the village headman.
sentences.s450-s24: "What will you do with these jewels now, that they may serve as a guide to your knowledge?"
sentences.s450-s29: They led Darab aside; they left nothing as a load except jewels and gold.
sentences.s450-s30: He traversed that frontier for sixty parasangs; in another city he built a place and settled.
sentences.s450-s31: In the foreign city he built inner quarters, in such a way that the village headman was made richer.
sentences.s450-s32: In a city whose chief was ill‑famed, that chief sent him a jewel.
sentences.s450-s33: She bound up garments and silver and gold from it, thus so that many gems would not remain.
sentences.s450-s34: In the house, except for some red brimstone, there remained nothing in the chest, neither bad nor good.
sentences.s450-s35: The woman, who reported the matter, became a guide; thus she said one day to the village headman.
sentences.s450-s36: “For we are free of need from this action; having become wealthy, do not go about your trade,”
sentences.s450-s13: A passing woman saw that and remained transfixed; she immediately cried out to the Creator.
sentences.s450-s14: She saw a radiant face shining among the silk, in appearance like Ardashir.
sentences.s450-s15: Beside his pillow were vessels of pleasant drink, and beneath him lay agate and peridot.
sentences.s450-s16: On his left hand there was a red dinar; on his right a ruby fit for a king.
sentences.s450-s45: He said to him, "This countryman's garment is like a stone; there is no shame in your earning a living."
sentences.s450-s46: When Darab fled that profession, Gazar constantly shed blood from his eyes.
sentences.s450-s47: His fortunes turned so that, for two purposes, you sought a trace of him both on the plain and in the city.
sentences.s450-s48: In one place you saw a bow in hand; according to custom the hand was open and the thumb closed.
sentences.s450-s1: When at dawn the passer came from the river, his companion said to him, “This is a greeting.”
sentences.s450-s2: Since you returned and your garments are somewhat damp, by this deed from whom will you obtain a dirham?
sentences.s450-s3: The passer's heart was withered from pain; one child in an undershirt had died.
sentences.s450-s4: The passer's wife, grieving for the young child, had withdrawn; her countenance darkened and her spirit fled.
sentences.s450-s49: You strung (or drew) your bow, spoke coldly, and ran off, saying, “O Parziyan, wolf fierce and quarrelsome!”
sentences.s450-s50: Why do you go about continually with arrow and bow? Why have you become suspicious over small matters?
sentences.s450-s51: He said thus to the passer: “O my father, why do you darken this my water?”
sentences.s450-s52: Give me to the learned (men) from the first; as I was taught, the Zand and the Esta are (to be) correct.
sentences.s450-s37: Thus the village-head gave him this answer: "O pure pair and guide."
sentences.s450-s38: You keep practising your craft — what more? The craft is always ahead of every deed.
sentences.s450-s39: Keep Darab pure and good; know what fortune will bring.
sentences.s450-s40: They regarded him so highly that from the fierce wind no harm was seen.
sentences.s450-s25: Gāzar said to the woman, “O good mate, in what earth and in what gem did you hide me?”
sentences.s450-s26: Let us at once leave this city and, away from narrowness and hardship, go to the plain.
sentences.s450-s27: To a city where no one knows us, where we are despised and no consoling/helping hand is at hand.
sentences.s450-s28: At night the passer took up a bundle, went off, and left no remembrance of home and homeland.
sentences.s450-s53: After that, make command your craft and seek it; now do not seek this village‑headship from me.
sentences.s450-s54: To him a man named Gazar recounted many things; thereafter he entrusted them to his instructors.
sentences.s450-s55: He learned culture and became of noble disposition; reproach arose from the message.
sentences.s450-s56: To that nurturer he said, “O father, no able worker will come from me.”
sentences.s450-s41: When the wheel (of fortune) turned away from his side for several years, one child came into being — a single child, with splendour and a mane.
sentences.s450-s42: He took to wrestling with the great men of the quarter; there was no one whose body and strength matched his.
sentences.s450-s43: All the fellow-children came together; suddenly they grew weary of him.
sentences.s450-s44: News of his deeds rose up as an outcry; indeed his once-brisk market grew dark.
sentences.s450-s57: Spare me your favour; without hesitation make riding in the world my profession.
sentences.s450-s58: The passer looked; the rider was complete — twisting the reins, setting the steed aglow, and well‑reputed.
sentences.s450-s59: He entrusted him to that man for a long time; he was taught everything needed for that dire necessity.
sentences.s450-s60: Having reins, spear and shield, he took his gear to the arena.
sentences.s450-s17: The woman quickly gave him her milk-bearing breast; the delightful child became glad because of that.
sentences.s450-s18: Because of that child's goodness and the fulfillment of his wish, his heart was cleansed of sorrow.
sentences.s450-s19: The passerby said to him that we should 'buy' this with our lives, so that it may last forever.
sentences.s450-s20: For this child would be renowned, even if he were a sovereign in the world.
